Q1: At 28, I've learned to be strategic with my time since I'm balancing a lot. I typically carved out chunks of about 20 hours per week, spreading them across evenings and weekends when I could focus. I'm a visual learner, so I'd organize my materials by creating diagrams and color-coded notes. My routine wasn't always perfect, but I tried to front-load watching lecture videos early in the week so I had time to process everything visually before diving into assignments.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I could stick to a Tuesday-Thursday-Sunday rhythm, but when deadlines piled up or life got complicated, I'd have to be flexible. With limited resources, I sometimes had to adjust my study times around when I could access reliable internet at the library or a café. I've learned to be resilient about adapting rather than beating myself up when the schedule falls apart.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was new video content to watch or when I wanted to check if there were discussion posts to respond to. Sometimes it was just that nagging feeling that I should check what I might be missing. The peer support group we had also motivated me to log in more regularly because I wanted to see what others were sharing. Honestly, the visual layout of new content notifications drew me in too.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, checking the LMS became like checking my email in the morning. The peer support intervention really helped with that because I felt accountable to my study group. We'd coordinate through the discussion boards, so I naturally logged in more frequently. It became less of a chore and more of a habit, especially once I realized the videos were actually helpful for my visual learning style.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I absorb information so much better visually. I spent a lot of time on those, probably close to 280 minutes total. The discussion boards were also valuable because connecting with peers helped me see concepts from different angles. When someone would explain something with a diagram or visual example, it just clicked for me in ways that text-heavy readings never did.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete as many of the smaller tasks as I should have, maybe only about a third of them. Some of the text-heavy supplementary readings I'd skim or skip because they weren't matching my learning style. When resources are tight and time is limited, you have to prioritize what actually helps you learn versus what feels like busywork. I focused my energy on the assignments that counted most for my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to what I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand something better. Visual content, interactive elements, or discussions where peers shared real insights, those felt valuable. If something was just reading for the sake of reading without a clear purpose, I'd question whether my limited study hours were better spent elsewhere. At my age, I've learned to be selective.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. When you're juggling five online courses with limited resources, you have to make hard choices. Required activities got my attention first, obviously. But some optional peer discussions actually felt more valuable than required readings because they were more engaging and helped me connect with my study group. It really depended on the format more than the requirement status.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download videos when I had good internet access and then watch them later offline, sometimes multiple times. I created my own visual study guides, mind maps, and diagrams that I'd review on paper because staring at screens all day is exhausting. I also used some free educational resources I found online to supplement concepts that weren't clicking. A lot of my actual learning happened with printed notes spread across my kitchen table.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd be sitting with my hand-drawn diagrams and notes, really working through problems, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd watch a downloaded video three times, pausing and sketching out concepts, but the system only registered one view. My peer study sessions happened over messaging apps, not the LMS discussion boards. The deep thinking and synthesis work I did offline was probably my most meaningful learning.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video or PDF once, download it, and then that material would become my primary study resource for days. With my internet situation being what it is, downloading was essential. I'd annotate PDFs, create visual summaries, and review them repeatedly offline. The LMS would show one quick access, but that material might have been the foundation of ten hours of study.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, usually when multiple deadlines converged across my five courses or when money was tight and stress was high. My attendance dropped to around 63% partly because of these overwhelming moments. But I've developed resilience over the years, so I'd reach out to my peer support group and find ways to push through. The surprise of getting good assignment scores despite everything kept me motivated to continue.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log in but just passively scroll rather than actively engage. Competing responsibilities, like work and life stuff, sometimes meant I'd have to choose between attending a live session or completing an assignment. I prioritized the high-stakes work. The LMS probably showed me logging in during those times, but my brain wasn't fully present. Fatigue made the visual content even more important because it required less cognitive effort than dense readings.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open materials and my mind would just wander. I'd have a video playing but be mentally elsewhere, worrying about bills or other courses. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything. Those sessions felt unproductive even though technically I was 'active' in the system. I learned to recognize when I was in that state and would step away rather than waste time pretending to study.
Q15: I postponed some of the smaller tasks and assignments, which is why my completion rate was only around 2% for certain things. It wasn't that I didn't know they mattered, but when you're stretched thin, you triage. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but sometimes later never came. The peer support group actually helped me avoid this pattern more than I would have on my own because I didn't want to let them down.
Q16: Deadlines were my primary drivers for LMS engagement. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly my login frequency would spike. The week before a major assignment, I'd be on the platform constantly, reviewing materials, checking rubrics, participating in discussions to clarify requirements. Without deadlines, I might have drifted more. They created urgency that helped me focus my limited time and energy on what mattered most.
Q17: Feedback was actually really motivating for me. When I saw my assignment scores coming back strong, like in the 90s, it surprised me and pushed me to maintain that level. It validated that my study strategies were working despite not doing every little task. Peer feedback in discussions also shaped how I engaged because I wanted to contribute meaningfully. Good feedback created a positive cycle that kept me coming back.
Q18: The course structure influenced me a lot. When modules were clearly organized with visual elements and logical progression, I engaged more deeply. The peer support component was particularly influential because it created social accountability. However, when the structure was unclear or content was buried in text-heavy formats, I'd disengage. Having five courses meant I gravitated toward the ones with clearer, more visual organization.
Q19: The video content made engagement much easier for someone like me who learns visually. The discussion boards were well-designed for peer interaction. What made it harder was when important information was hidden in long text documents or when the navigation wasn't intuitive. Also, having required tasks scattered unpredictably through the course made it easy to miss things. A clear visual roadmap of what was due when would have helped enormously.
Q20: I think that pattern describes me somewhat. I was highly engaged, logging in frequently, watching tons of video content, participating in discussions, but my task completion was only around 33%. For me, it was about strategic prioritization. I focused on understanding the material deeply rather than checking every box. Sometimes high activity reflects genuine learning that doesn't translate into submitted work because of time constraints or strategic choices about what matters most.
Q21: That definitely resonates with my experience. My exam score was solid and I earned a Merit despite moderate attendance and low task completion. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show in the LMS. I think students who know how to learn efficiently can extract what they need from materials without constant platform engagement. Quality of engagement matters more than quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like when students are most active, which resources they access, and participation in discussions. But they miss the depth of engagement, the offline study, the thinking that happens away from the screen. They can't capture whether someone really understood a video or just let it play. For visual learners like me who download and study materials offline, logs significantly underrepresent actual engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, not just showing up. It's when I'm creating those visual diagrams, when I'm explaining concepts to my peer group, when I'm connecting new ideas to what I already know. It's not about login counts or time on platform. Real engagement is when learning changes how you think. For me, it often happened in quiet moments with my notes spread out, far from any LMS.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how the peer support intervention genuinely changed my experience and kept me motivated during tough times. It wouldn't capture my hand-drawn study materials or the hours I spent reviewing downloaded content. It wouldn't show the resilience I've built over years of overcoming obstacles, or how surprised and encouraged I felt when my hard work paid off in good grades despite limited resources. The human story behind the data points is invisible.
